title: Agent-based model demonstrates the impact of nonlinear, complex interactions between cytokines on muscle regeneration
idea_synthesis: We develop a spatial agent-based model (ABM) of murine skeletal muscle regeneration in the Cellular-Potts framework to investigate how nonlinear, combinatorial perturbations of cytokine decay and diffusion parameters shape cross-sectional area (CSA) recovery, satellite stem cell (SSC) dynamics, fibroblast proliferation, macrophage polarization, and microvascular remodeling over 28 days after acute injury.

sections.name: Introduction
goals: Motivate muscle injury as a common orthopedic problem with limited treatments
Introduce the complexity of cytokine networks during regeneration
Explain why in silico, spatial modeling is required given >1 million possible cytokine combinations
State goals: develop spatial ABM with microvasculature, calibrate, validate, perform in silico perturbations

sections.name: Related Work
goals: Review prior ABMs of muscle regeneration and their limitations
Discuss Cellular-Potts framework origins
Contrast non-spatial vs. spatial cytokine representations
Highlight gap: lack of microvascular adaptation and dynamic ECM properties

sections.name: Methods
goals: Describe ABM architecture (CompuCell3D, 2D fascicle cross-section, 50 fibers, 28 days, 15-min mcs, 45 CPM evaluations)
Enumerate agent types and cytokines (7 cytokines)
Detail rule counts per agent type
Explain CaliPro parameter density estimation (600 LHS samples, 8 iterations, 100 replicates)
LHS-PRCC sensitivity analysis (300 samples, Bonferroni-corrected)
Validation strategy via held-out cell counts and perturbation comparison

sections.name: Results
goals: Calibration / validation: model outputs track experimental CSA, SSC, fibroblast counts; 95% CI within SD except SSC day 3
Perturbation validation vs. published experiments (VEGF-A dose response, cell depletions, hindered angiogenesis, IL-10 KO)
Nonlinear / synergistic cytokine insights from in silico experiments
LHS-PRCC: HGF decay most influential; TGF-beta and MMP-9 decay positively correlated with CSA
Combined cytokine perturbation: 13% CSA improvement at 28 days

sections.name: Discussion
goals: Interpret 13% CSA gain mechanism: early SSC boost + late anti-inflammatory shift
Connect to translational cytokine delivery strategies (PRP, plasmin activation, IL-4 biomaterials, anti-IL-6)
Advances over prior ABMs: spatial cytokine diffusion, microvasculature, CaliPro
Nonlinear thresholds (VEGF-A dose, non-monotonic TNF-alpha fibroblast response)
Limitations: missing cell types, no hypertrophy, 2D cross-section, male-only calibration
Future work: exogenous cytokine scheduling, sex differences, personalized injury models

queries: Latin hypercube sampling partial rank correlation biological model
HGF hepatocyte growth factor satellite cell activation
platelet rich plasma muscle injury therapy
Duchenne muscular dystrophy agent based model

table_plan.purpose: Summary of calibration targets and pass criteria (CSA, SSC, fibroblast)
Perturbation validation outcomes vs. published experiments

figure_plan.caption: ABM agents, cytokines, and interaction overview
Calibration pass rate / model fit to experimental data

figure_plan.caption: CSA recovery improvement across cytokine perturbations at 28 days
figure_plan.data_source: 13% improvement for combined perturbation vs. unaltered (log verbatim)

anti_leakage_notes: Do not use original biorxiv DOI 2023.08.14.553247 or figure identifiers.
Reference numbers from original paper (e.g., 76, 97, 102) are NOT valid citations; all cites must be re-verified.
Only the explicit 13% and 30% values appear verbatim in log.
